coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional green-emitting fluorescent carbon dots: A versatile fluorometric probe for glyphosate detection and applications in food
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sasikumar, Thangarasu
Packialakshmi, Jeyakumar Saranya
Hong, Su Jung
Ha, Seong Yong
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: In the study, dissolvable green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) were synthesized utilizing 4-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) precursors through a single-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized G-CDs were characterized using UV–visible, fluorescence, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), fluorescence lifetime, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and zeta potential studies. HR-TEM analysis revealed spherical G-CDs with a uniform particle size of ∼ 4.4 nm. The prepared G-CDs showed remarkable stability under varying physiological properties, photostability, and diverse fluorescence properties in a variety of solvents. The solvatochromic behavior has been exploited to detect water molecules in organic solvents. The G-CDs emitted bright green fluorescence at 513 nm when excited at 370 nm and were used as a fluorometric probe for glyphosate (GLY) detection. Selective quenching of G-CDs fluorescence by GLY was confirmed through Stern-Volmer and time-resolved fluorescence analysis. The calculated limit of detection for GLY was 0.044 μM. These probes exhibited excellent selectivity towards GLY even in the presence of other interfering compounds. These properties of G-CDs have also been utilized to detect a GLY herbicide in real food applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensor
Glyphosate
Solvatochromism
Food samples analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The formation mechanism process of G-CDs
Evaluation of fluorescence solvatochromic properties of G-CDs
Detection and mechanism of GLY by using G-CDs as a fluorescent probe
Fluorescence detection of real samples analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (RS-2023\u201300262394) and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022M3H4A1A03085361).

This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (RS-2023-00262394) and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022M3H4A1A03085361).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The absorption spectrum (a) and excitation and emission spectra (b) of G-CDs. Inset: the optical images of G-CDs under daylight (i) and a 365 nm UV lamp (ii).
HR-TEM images of G-CDs. Inset: lattice fringes of G-CDs (a & b), the particle size distribution of the G-CDs (c).
XPS survey (a), high-resolution C 1 s (b), N 1 s (c) and O 1 s (d) spectra of G-CDs.
(a) 1H NMR and (b) 13C NMR spectra of G-CDs in DMSO-d6.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the G-CDs in different solvents of water, ethanol, methanol, DMF, DMSO, acetonitrile, acetone, acetic acid toluene and chloroform. (b) Plot between the Stokes shift of the G-CDs and the solvent parameter ET N and (c) The photographic pictures of G-CDs in various solvents under visible and UV light 365 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of G-CDs in the ethanol solvent containing water contents from 0 % to 100 % (v/v), (b) plot of F0-F vs the water content, and (c) the photographic image of the G-CDs in ethanol containing different water contents. (Inset shows the photographs of G-CDs before (i) and after addition (ii) of water).

(a) Emission spectra of G-CDs with increasing concentrations of GLY 3.33–50 μM, (b) the plot of relative emission intensity (ΔF) vs different concentrations of GLY (3.33–50 μM). (Insets display photographic pictures of G-CDs before and after the addition of GLY (i & ii)), (c) Stern-Volmer plot for the G-CDs system in the presence of different concentrations of GLY (Inset: The linear ranges for GLY are from 3.3 to 26 µM, respectively) and (d) lifetime spectra of G-CDs in the absence and presence of GLY.
(a & b) The selectivity of G-CDs for the detection of GLY with and without the other interfering compounds.
Schematic illustration showing the preparation process of G-CDs based on the fluorescence detection of GLY and their multifunctional applications.
A plausible mechanism of the formation of G-CDs from the precursors of p-PDA and GABA.
Ascertainment of GLY in three real-time food samples by standard addition method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Multifunctional green-emitting fluorescent carbon dots: A versatile fluorometric probe for glyphosate detection and applications in food

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the study, dissolvable green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) were synthesized utilizing 4-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) precursors through a single-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized G-CDs were characterized using UV–visible, fluorescence, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), fluorescence lifetime, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and zeta potential studies. HR-TEM analysis revealed spherical G-CDs with a uniform particle size of ∼ 4.4 nm. The prepared G-CDs showed remarkable stability under varying physiological properties, photostability, and diverse fluorescence properties in a variety of solvents. The solvatochromic behavior has been exploited to detect water molecules in organic solvents. The G-CDs emitted bright green fluorescence at 513 nm when excited at 370 nm and were used as a fluorometric probe for glyphosate (GLY) detection. Selective quenching of G-CDs fluorescence by GLY was confirmed through Stern-Volmer and time-resolved fluorescence analysis. The calculated limit of detection for GLY was 0.044 μM. These probes exhibited excellent selectivity towards GLY even in the presence of other interfering compounds. These properties of G-CDs have also been utilized to detect a GLY herbicide in real food applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of the general world population has led to a continuously increasing demand to meet qualitative and quantitative needs for food and agricultural products [1,2]. To overcome and meet these expectations in agriculture, farmers have chosen herbicides, pesticides, and fungicides in agricultural cultivation [2,3]. Additionally, chemicals are often used to manage and eradicate pests in agricultural fields, livestock farms, and residential areas. The main role of pesticides is to improve the quality and yield of agricultural products (e.g., vegetables, fruits, herbs, and crops) by reducing crop damage caused by weeds and insects [4,5]. Insects can also damage the food crops during the production, processing, packaging, storage, and sale of vegetables, fruits, agro-food products, and related products. Pest control in bulk storage and distribution facilities requires chemical control in the form of pesticides. Long-term consumption of high concentrations of pesticides affecting food can cause various chronic complications, including neurological disorders, diabetes, asthma, cancer, and even death [2,6–8]. Therefore, it is essential to develop effective and sensitive sensing techniques to find pesticide residues in food and the environment.

Glyphosate (GLY) (N-(phosphonomethyl) glycine) is widely used as a herbicide in the agricultural sector to manage weed growth in various crops. It is also abundant in the environment due to its high-water solubility [9,10]. First released by Monsanto in 1970 under the brand name Roundup, the GLY has grown to become one of the most used herbicides worldwide [11]. It is currently used worldwide and its residues are found in nearly 750 products used in agroforestry sectors. Although early studies demonstrated that GLY has relatively little toxicity to mammalian cells, more recent studies have shown that overuse of GLY has negative environmental impacts. On the other hand, long-term residues from GLY use in agriculture harm the environment and pose health risks. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has approved a maximum contaminate limit of 0.7 ppm for GLY in drinking water [12]. This makes it more important than ever to monitor GLY levels in drinking water, fruits, vegetables, and crops. Therefore, precise analytical methods are required for the accurate determination of GLY in drinking water, agricultural products, vegetables, fruits, and ecological samples. GLY in various matrices can be detected using various techniques such as HPLC [13], gas chromatography [14] electrochemical methods [15,16], capillary electrophoresis [17], mass spectrometry [18] and colorimetry [19] have been employed for GLY detection across diverse matrices. While these methods offer accuracy and sensitivity, they are often associated with drawbacks such as the requirement for costly and sophisticated instrumentation, skilled operators, complicated sample preparation procedures, and prolonged testing durations. Consequently, there is a critical need to develop rapid, accurate, and on-side detection approaches for GLY.

In recent years, CDs have gained much interest due to their facile synthesis, peculiar size-dependent photophysical characteristics, high chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity compared with other nanomaterials [20–27]. These intrinsic properties of CDs have resulted in widespread application in various felids, such as fluorescence imaging, drug release, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, energy conversation, anti-counterfeiting of CDs, including fluorescent chemotherapy, biosensors, etc. [28–34]. CDs commonly utilized in sensing applications typically exhibit blue fluorescence but are characterized by lower quantum yield [29,35–37]. Therefore, development of G-CDs with improved quantum yield and phtostability is still getting attention to realize potential applications.

In this study, a facile and direct synthesis of highly green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) was proposed for the specific detection of GLY ( Scheme 1). The structure and formation of the G-CDs were determined through FT-IR and XPS and NMR analysis. The solvatochromism properties of G-CDs were investigated for fluorometric turn-off detection of water in an organic solvent (ethanol). Additionally, the distinctive fluorescent characteristics of G-CDs were harnessed for the development of a highly responsive fluorometric turn-off probe for the quantitative determination of GLY. The G-CDs probe has been successfully used to detect GLY in real food and water samples.

Gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA), and Glyphosate (GLY) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals (St. Louis, MO, USA). Additional interfering compounds were purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea). All compounds used were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Experimental glassware was meticulously cleaned using freshly prepared aqua regia and rinsed with double distilled water. Phosphate buffer solution and double-distilled water were used and all analyzes were performed at 25 °C room temperature.

G-CDs were prepared based on a previously reported method with minor modifications [38]. Briefly, p-PDA (0.32 g) and GABA (0.30 g) were completely dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, sonicated for 20 min, and the clear solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon cylinder and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Afterwards, the autoclave was cooled, the G-CDs solution was collected, and the collected samples were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min to collect the supernatant. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm pore membrane filter (Whatman International Ltd., Maidstone, England). The obtained G-CDs solution was refrigerated in a dark room.

The freshly prepared GLY solution was stored at refrigerated conditions. Fluorescence measurements were performed by mixing different GLY concentrations with 3.0 mL of deionized water and 200 µL of G-CDs. After incubation for 10 min, emission spectral analysis was performed on the solutions. The GLY detection assay was performed in triplicate and the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

The cucumber and lettuce were obtained from a local market in Seoul, Korea. The collected samples were ground in a juicer with the addition of 100 mL water. The sample solution was sonicated and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min to obtain the supernatant. These real samples were spiked with various known concentrations of GLY. The solution was added to the solution containing G-CDs and analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy. The recovery rate is calculated using Eq. (1) [39].
(1)
Rate of recovery ( % ) =
Amount of GLY found Amount of GLY added
× 100

The optical characteristics of G-CDs in aqueous dispersions were thoroughly investigated using UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectroscopy measurements. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the absorption spectrum of G-CDs exhibited two distinct absorption bands at 280 and 370 nm. The band at 280 nm is associated with the core state of G-CDs and represents a higher energy level indicative of π-π* transition within sp2 domains. In contrast, the broad absorption band at 370 nm was attributed to the surface state of G-CDs, which consists of a collection of low-energy absorption bands. These are probably caused by the various functional groups present on the G-CDs surface, indicating their significant role in the optical properties of these G-CDs [38,40].

The fluorescence characteristics of G-CDs were assessed through steady-state fluorescence spectroscopic investigations and the excitation and emission spectra profiles are depicted in Fig. 1(b). Under excitation at 370 nm, G-CDs displayed a noticeable emission peak at 513 nm, signifying the formation of carbon dots (G-CDs) with bright green fluorescence. Moreover, when the emission was set at 513 nm, G-CDs exhibited an excitation band at 370 nm. The highest emission band of G-CDs remains constant throughout a range of excitation wavelengths from 360 to 405 nm, and the fluorescence maximum of the G-CDs was observed at 513 nm. Notably, the emission maximum of G-CDs was independent of the excitation wavelength from 360 to 405 nm (Fig. S1) (supplementary). The inset of Fig. 1(b) (i & ii) illustrates that the aqueous dispersion of G-CDs appears light brown in visible light but exhibits green fluorescence when exposed to UV light. The quantum yield of the synthesized G-CDs was 17.5 % using quinine sulfate as a reference [35]. The long-term stability of G-CDs is shown in Fig. S2 (supplementary). The maximum emission wavelength of G-CDs did not change and the stability of the G-CDs was found to be stable for more than 3 months, as evidenced by the undisturbed emission spectrum and bright green color of G-CDs.

HR-TEM was used to characterize the size and morphological distribution of G-CDs and the results are shown in Fig. 2. It is evident from the HR-TEM micrographs of G-CDs that the particles have uniform sizes and almost spherical appearances without any obvious agglomerations (Fig. 2(a&b)). HR-TEM micrograph showed that the average particle size of G-CDs was approximately 4.4 nm (Fig. 2(c)). The HR-TEM micrograph of G-CDs clearly shows lattice fringes in the core region as shown Fig. 2(b) inset. The measured interlayer d spacing between lattice fringes is 0.18 nm, which is reliable for the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. These results show that G-CDs have a crystalline graphite core and an amorphous outer carbon layer [41].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was utilized to investigate the surfaces of G-CDs. The XPS survey spectrum shows the composition of the G-CDs mainly carbon (75.67 %), nitrogen (15.9 %) and oxygen (8.43 %) as shown in Fig. 3(a-d). Additionally, the identified peaks for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s were observed at 283.46, 397.92, and 530.25 (Fig. 3a), respectively [38]. The detailed C 1 s XPS spectrum displayed four distinct peaks at 283.46 eV (C−C/CC), 285.9 eV (C−N), and 286.0 eV (C−O) (Fig. 3(b)). The N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 397.9, 399.5, and 401.1 eV, correlating with pyridine C−NC, N−C, and graphitic N−C3, respectively (Fig. 3(c)). The O1s XPS spectrum showed clear peaks for C−OH and CO at 530.25 and 532.3 eV (Fig. 3(d)). Moreover, the XRD pattern of G-CDs showed a graphite-like structure with a peak at 24° (Fig. S3, supplementary).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to determine the surface functional group of G-CDs, GABA and p-PDA are shown in Fig. S4 (supplementary). The FT-IR analyzes the unique distinctive vibration signals for each compound. For GABA, numerous characteristics of stretching and bending vibrations were observed for –CH2, O-H, and N–H groups at specific wavenumbers (882, 1380, and 1508 cm− 1, respectively), along with C-OH and CO stretching vibrations at 1043 and 1684 cm−1. The C-H stretching vibrations were observed at 2881 and 2972 cm−1, with collective stretching vibration of NH2 and COOH groups at 3315 cm−1 [40]. FT-IR spectrum of p-PDA shows bands at 3370 and 3298 cm−1, attributed to O-H and N-H stretching of coordinated water molecules, with the N-H bending mode of the NH2 group identified at 1627 cm−1. Moreover, the aromatic vibrations associated with C−N, C−H, and CC are responsible for the bands at 1251, 3007, and 1506 cm−1, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of p-PDA shows bands at 3370 and 3298 cm−1, corresponding to O-H and N−H of coordinated water molecules, respectively [41–43]. The N-H bending mode of the NH2 group is responsible for the strong peak observed at 1627 cm−1. The aromatic vibrations of C−N, C−H, and CC are responsible for the bands at 1251, 3007, and 1506 cm−1, respectively [42–44]. The FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs shows wide absorption bands at 3222–3321 and 2892–2953 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O−H/N−H and C−H, respectively [38]. The sharp absorption peak at 1659 cm−1 represents the carboxyl (COOH) group, Additionally, the peaks at 1508 and 1468 cm−1 are associated with stretching vibration of CC and C−N [38]. These findings suggest that NH2 groups are likely present on the surface of the G-CDs to impart, a negative charge, as confirmed by the zeta potential of −9.68 mV (Fig. S5, supplementary), implying the presence of negatively charged surface functional groups.

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of G-CDs were recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 4. The 1H NMR spectrum of G-CDs showed chemical shifts at δ = 3.67 and 4.36 ppm corresponding to the hydrogen vibration of -NH or -CH2 moieties. The chemical shifts observed at 2.41 ppm corresponds to the proton. Additionally, the singlet observed at δ = 6.15, and the doublets at δ = 6.45, 6.63, 6.88, 7.28, and 7.62 ppm are attributed to aromatic protons. The 13C NMR spectrum showed ethyl (CH2) carbon signals at δ = 18.06, 21.00, 28.62, 30.54, 32.58, 42.06, and 42.07 ppm and aromatic carbon signals at δ = 114.18, 114.37, 114.92, 116.16, 122.18, 124.04, 124.42, 129.47, 139.45, 143.85, 145.79, 146.00, and 151.49 ppm, respectively (Fig. 4(b)). The carbonyl (CO) signals at δ = 178.16 and 175.70, and a carbonitrile (CN) signal at δ = 173.50 ppm are attributed to the presence of aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms [41]. For comparison, Fig. S6(a&b) and Fig. S7(a&b) (supplementary) showed the 1H NMR spectra of p-PDA and GABA. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of G-CDs showed characteristics of both p-PDA and GABA, suggesting that the functional groups of the precursor molecules are effectively incorporated in G-CDs [45,46].

Steady-state fluorescence spectroscopic measurements of G-CDs were performed to investigate the effects of different physiological states, including various pH levels, irradiation times, and salt concentrations (Fig. S8(a-d)) (supplementary). The effect of changing pH from 1 to 13 on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs is shown in Fig. S8(a) (supplementary). High fluorescence intensity was observed in near neutral and basic conditions (pH 7–13), but a weak fluorescence intensity was observed in acidic conditions (pH 1–5). This suggests that G-CDs can perform better as a detection probe at pH 7 of PBS solutions and was therefore selected for subsequent GLY detection experiments. The change in the photophysical behavior of G-CDs between acidic and basic conditions is due to the change in surface charge, where protonation occurs under acidic conditions, and deprotonation occurs under basic conditions, thereby, increasing the emission intensity. Previous studies have shown that the surface charge and fluorescence intensity of G-CDs fluctuate upon protonation and deprotonation at different pH levels [44]. The emission spectrum of G-CDs was found to be temperature dependent within the range of 25, 40, 60 and 80 °C (Fig. S8(b), supplementary). The fluorescence intensity of G-CDs decreased with increasing temperature. Based on the analysis results, room temperature was determined to be the optimal condition to evaluate the fluorescence behavior of G-CDs. The photostability of G-CDs was approached by continuously irradiating G-CDs with UV light for 60 min. As shown in Fig. S8(c) (supplementary), the fluorescence intensity remained without significant changes, indicating the excellent photostability of G-CDs. Additionally, different concentrations of NaCl did not significantly change the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs (Fig. S8(d), supplementary), indicating that G-CDs are relatively salt-tolerant and highly stable under these conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation mechanism process of G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A possible formation mechanism of G-CDs from the starting precursors and GABA was proposed ( Scheme 2). During the hydrothermal reaction, p-PDA and GABA form a combination of random copolymers and uniform polymers. It is believed that near the reaction temperature of p-PDA and GABA, acid amides are initially formed, followed by random polymer formation (step-I) [45]. In another pathway, step-II, p-PDA easily forms stable polymer chains in the presence of high pressure and temperature. These chains are subsequently carbonized and cross-linked to produce the final G-CDs with a polymer structure. The FT-IR, XPS NMR spectra analysis reveal that G-CDs include an abundance of functional groups, such as amines, amides, and hydroxyls (Fig. S4 (supplementary), Fig. 3(a-d), Fig. 4(a,b), Fig. S6(a&b) and S7(a&b) (supplementary). The fluorescence of G-CDs depends on these functional groups on their surfaces [45,47,48]. It has been reported that the CDs resulting from step-I, could exhibit heterogeneous composition with random copolymer formation, thereby leading to the excitation wavelength dependent emission properties in CDs. Conversely, in step-II, the CDs could exhibit the formation of uniform polymer chains, resulting in a homogeneous structure with excitation independent emission properties suggesting the existence of singluar emission center arising from uniform polymer chains. Thus, it can be inferred that the formation of CDs from PDA and GABA take place by means of step-II reaction pathway [49]. The nitrogen composition of G-CDs can produce various surface structures such as pyridinic, amino, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogens. The incorporation of GABA into CDs results in the disappearance of amino N and reduction of pyrrolic N, which is associated with a change in the emission state and a significant increase in green emission due to an increase in pyridine N. In a recent study, Yuan et al. [49] proposed that in CDs derived from p-PDA, the red emission originates from the fluorophore formed by the deformation of p-PDA, while the green emission is attributed to pyridinic N. Thus, based on the aforementioned analyses, it can be inferred that the presence of graphitic N, pyridine N, oxygen atoms, and the fluorophore formed by the deformation of p-PDA on the particle surfaces collectively contribute to the red emission observed in CDs [49]. This suggests that pyridine N is primarily responsible for the green emission. From this discussion, it becomes clear that the luminescence of CDs occurs mainly from the surface state and that tunable nitrogen composition is a key factor affecting the change in luminescence properties [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of fluorescence solvatochromic properties of G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission properties of G-CDs were investigated in various solvents such as water, ethanol, methanol, dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetonitrile, acetone, acetic acid, toluene, and chloroform (Fig. S9, supplementary). Additionally, solvent polarity also affects the emissions of synthesized G-CDs. As shown in Fig. S9(a, b) (supplementary), the absorption spectra and photographic images of G-CDs dispersed in various solvents. Distinct absorption bands are absorbed at 370, 373, 372, 377, 375, 372, 398, 380, 358, and 360 nm respectively, indicating that G-CDs exhibit different surface states in different chemical environments. These changes in surface states suggest that they play an important role in the emission process. The solvent-dependent emission properties of G-CDs were investigated to observe the emission wavelengths in different solvents and at different excitation wavelength (350–430 nm) (Fig. S9(c-k), supplementary). When excited at 370 nm, G-CDs dispersed in these solvents exhibit a distinct emission peak maximum between 433 and 513 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(a). This solvent effect is also evident in the absorption spectra, with the maximum absorption peak shifting to shorter wavelengths as solvent polarity increases (Fig. S9(a)). Additionally, there are emission peak (λem), absorption peak (λabs), and stokes shift of G-CDs with respect to the solvent polarity parameter (ET N) [21]. The stokes shift shows a correlation with the ET N solvent polarity parameter. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the stokes shifts are much larger in protic solvents compared to aprotic solvents. For G-CDs, the solvent-dependent emission properties can be attributed to dipole-induced perturbations of the surface electronic states. As listed in Table S1 (supplementary), increasing solvent polarity causes a significant shift of the emission spectrum of CDs from the blue to the red end, suggesting the dipole-induced fluorescence changes [21]. For example, G-CDs disperse in water, ethanol, methanol, dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide, acetonitrile, acetone, acetic acid, toluene, and chloroform to produce green, orange, light purple, dirty white olive green, and cyan blue under visible and UV light (365 nm), as shown in Fig. 5(c). G-CDs in water emitted green fluorescence, whereas G-CDs in other solvents did not show significant fluorescence in a similar manner. Notably, G-CDs in solvents such as acetone and acetic acid, emitted only weak fluorescence, and did not produce strong fluorescence under UV light. This may be attributed to the pH effect [51,52]. In general, as solvent polarity increases, the emission peak tends to shift toward the red end of the emission spectrum. This solvent-dependent phenomenon is very similar to the concept of "solvatochromism" observed in organic dyes. Many organic dyes exhibit shifts in their fluorescence emissions when placed in different solvents. This solvatochromic effect is typically attributed to intramolecular charge transfer processes. This effect is common in organic dyes but is rarely known in CDs. The ethanol solvent mixed G-CDs exhibited bright orange emission with a significantly increased intensity of emission when compared to the G-CDs dispersed in water. Furthermore, the relative quantum yield of G-CDs in ethanol was higher than that measured for the G-CDs in water [41].

For the emission behavior of specific solvents such as water and ethanol, fluorescence lifetime analysis was performed for G-CDs in both water and ethanol solutions. As shown in Fig. S10 (supplementary), fluorescence lifetime analysis of G-CDs in water and ethanol accurately described the triple exponential method with an average lifetime of 2.27 and 3.33 ns, respectively. In particular, the longer lifetime of G-CDs in ethanol increases the quantum yield. To further understand the fluorescence mechanism of G-CDs in water and ethanol, the radiative K r and nonradiative K nr rate constants were calculated from the average lifetime (τ) and quantum yield (ϕ) using the following equations [41,52].
Kᵣ = ϕ/τ
1/τ = Kᵣ + Kₙᵣ

It can be seen that Knr (8.63 × 108 s −1) for G-CDs in water is significantly higher than Kr (1.36 × 108 s−1), indicating that the fluorescence mechanism is dominated by non-radiative rate processes. Compared to ethanol, Kr (2.13 × 108 s−1) is almost similar, but Knr is reduced to 7.86 × 108 s−1. All these observations indicate that the fluorescence increase of G-CDs in ethanol is mainly due to the suppression of the non-radiative recombination pathway [41,53].

Fig. 6(a) shows that G-CDs dispersed in ethanol exhibited a strong emission band with a maximum at 493 nm when exited at 370 nm. The emission band of G-CDs is constant in the range of excitation wavelength from 350–430 nm as shown in Fig. S9(c) (supplementary). With a gradual increase in the water content of the G-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity at 493 nm gradually decreased, no new emission band appeared, and a slight bathochromic was shifted with increasing water. Calibration curves for this phenomenon using various ratios of G-CDs fluorescence intensity to water are shown in Fig. 5(b). In ethanol, this curve has a linear range from 0 % to 100 % water content (R2 = 0.9955). The limit of detection (LOD) for water in ethanol was calculated using the formula LOD = 3 S/slope (where S is the standard deviation of the blank samples) and was determined to be 0.12 %. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the color of the G-CDs solution shifts from orange to green with increasing water concentration. This suggests that ratiometric fluorescence probes are effective for visual detection of water content in ethanol over a broad range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and mechanism of GLY by using G-CDs as a fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The selective detection of GLY was investigated using green fluorescence of G-CDs. The detection assay was performed by adding various concentrations of GLY to the aqueous dispersion of G-CDs solutions. After that, the fluorescence spectral profile of G-CDs was recorded in the absence and presence of GLY as shown in Fig. 7(a). G-CDs exhibit a strong emission peak at 512 nm in the absence of GLY. Interestingly, the gradual addition of GLY resulted in a continuous decrease in the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs without significant shifts in the emission band. The addition of 50.00 µM GLY reduced the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs by approximately 70 %, demonstrating the ability to selectively detect GLY. The degree of fluorescence quenching was directly correlated with GLY concentration. Utilizing this data from steady-state fluorescence analysis, a calibration plot was generated showing the relationship between relative emission intensity and GLY concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7(b), this plot shows a linear relationship with a strong correlation coefficient (0.9968) over the GLY concentration range from 3.33 to 50.00 µM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.044 µM. The performance of the present detection probe was compared with other fluorescence-based sensors for GLY (Table S2, supplementary). The LOD obtained with this method was similar to or relatively superior to those of previously reported fluorescence-based assays.

The process of fluorescence quenching in fluorophores involves mechanisms like fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), static or binding-related quenching, and dynamic quenching [54,55]. To elucidate the specific quenching mechanisms of G-CDs, the data obtained from fluorescence titration experiments were analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 2) [55].
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 + Ksv
Q
= 1
+
K
q
τ
0
Q
where F0 represents the initial fluorescence emission intensity of G-CDs, F denotes the emission intensity in the GLY presence, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Q is the concentration of GLY and
τ
0
is the average lifetime (2.27 ns) of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the Stern-Volmer plot shows a linear relationship between F0/F and concentrations of GLY in the range of 3.3–50 µM, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9854). In general, linear Stern-Volmer plots suggest a single type of quenching process, either static or dynamic. In this case, the quenching of G-CDs may occur due to static or dynamic mechanisms. The calculated Stern-Volmer constant Ksv for G-CDs containing GLY was 2.80 × 104 dm3 mol−1 s−1 and the quenching rate constant (kq) derived from kq = Ksv/
τ
0
was 2.80 × 1012 dm3 mol−1 s−1 (Table S3, supplementary). According to a previous report, the value of diffusion-controlled quenching is 1010 dm3 mol−1 s−1 [55]. Interestingly, the kq value in this study is larger than the typical diffusion-controlled quenching process, suggesting that the quenching behavior of G-CDs mainly involves static or radiative quenching mechanisms.

The lifetime analysis is an ideal tool to distinguish between static and FRET quenching mechanisms. A study of the average fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs before and after GLY addition is shown in Fig. 7(d). The decay profile of G-CDs in the initial excited state has a lifetime of approximately 2.27 ns. Adding GLY changes this lifetime little to approximately 2.37 ns. It is clear that the addition of GLY did not change the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs. This obviously demonstrates that a ground-state complex was formed between G-CDs and GLY. Additionally, the presence of GLY in G-CDs was further confirmed through zeta potential analysis as shown in Fig. S11 (supplementary). The addition of GLY to the pristine G-CDs significantly changed the zeta potential from −9.68 to −2.70 mV. This zeta potential indicates the complex formation of G-CD and GLY.

The fluorometric response of G-CDs toward different interfering compounds such as glyphosate, parathion methyl, carbaryl, omethoate, methomyl, carbendazim, chlorothalonil, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr2O7 2−, I−, NO3 −, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Fe2+ and Ag+ was evaluated by adding 0.001 M interfering compounds to the probe mixture. It is obvious from Fig. 8(a) that the fluorescence intensity ratio of G-CDs largely remained unperturbed in the presence of various interfering compounds (parathion methyl, carbaryl, omethoate, methomyl, carbendazim, chlorothalonil, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr2O7 2−, I−, NO3 −, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Fe2+ and Ag+) except for the significant changes seen for the addition of GLY. However, upon performing the fluorescence analysis of G-CDs at a lower concentration regime (3.33–50 μM) of this analyte, only the addition of GLY evoked a substantial fluorescence response. All these results show that G-CDs have a high specificity toward GLY over other interfering compounds, indicating that the G-CDs could be effectively used as a sensitive fluorometric probe for the detection of GLY. The interfering analytes, including pesticides, anions and cations with and without GLY, were added to the G-CDs solutions and the results are shown in Fig. 8(a & b). As shown in Fig. 8(a), the fluorescence response of G-CDs in the presence of higher concentrations (0.1 N) of interfering compounds such as parathion methyl, carbaryl, omethoate, methomyl, carbendazim, chlorothalonil, Al3+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cr2O7 2−, I−, NO3 −, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Ag+ these interferences had no effect in G-CDs on GLY detection. Continuously, the Cu2+ and Fe3+ interfering compounds did not change G-CDs fluorescence intensity on GLY detection in the presence of 0.01 N higher concentrations. Significant changes in the relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs are only associated with the addition of GLY. The results indicated that the current detection platform showed a high selectivity of G-CDs for GLY detection. The aforementioned studies suggest that the fluorescence response using this method is highly selective for GLY measurements. As shown in Fig. 8(b) (dark yellow and purple column), even in the presence of interference, the G-CDs system performs the same for GLY recognition, indicating that the present GLY detection system can effectively resist interfering samples. These results indicate that GLY can be detected with high selectivity using G-CDs, a label-free, rapid, and inexpensive sensor system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The utility of the G-CDs assay for practical applications was validated through the detection of GLY in real food and water samples. Initially, the collected samples were spiked with known quantities of GLY, and the spiked solution was mixed with the G-CDs dispersion. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was measured. The quantitative estimation of GLY in the food and water samples is shown in Table 1. From the results, it can be drawn that yield recoveries from the current approach ranged from 96.34 % to 101.22 % with lesser relative standard deviation (RSD) values.

In summary, the results obtained affirm that the developed G-CDs act as potential nanoprobe with improved sensitivity for GLY detection. The fluorescence properties of G-CDs demonstrate remarkable stability under various physiological conditions, including under varying pH, ionic strength and exposure to UV irradiation. G-CDs exhibited a specific fluorescence turn-off response to GLY due to the formation of ground-state complexes. Additionally, the G-CDs show unique fluorescence behaviors that are dependent on solvent polarity, a property that has been utilized to detect water content in organic solvents with a LOD of 0.12 %. The G-CDs nanoprobe showed excellent selectivity toward GLY even in the presence of other potential interfering compounds with LOD as low as 0.044 µM. The high sensitivity of the G-CDs was exploited to determine the herbicide detection in vegetables such as cucumber, lettuce, and in tap water. Unlike the several fluorophore and metal nanoparticle-based sensing methods reported previously, the synthesized G-CDs are highly sensitive with a broad linear range of detection. The sensitivity, simplicity, and reproducibility make the G-CDs a discreet option for the development of probes for GLY detection and in food safety applications.

Seong Yong Ha: Visualization, Methodology. Su Jung Hong: Visualization, Methodology. Jeyakumar Packialakshmi: Visualization, Methodology. Thangarasu Sasikumar: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Gye Hwa Shin: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (RS-2023–00262394) and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022M3H4A1A03085361).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.113356.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, C. Sun, X. Chen, Y. Zhang, V.L. Colvin, Q. Rice, et al., Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 1909-1915.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence solvatochromism of carbon dot dispersions prepared from phenylenediamine and optimization of red emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Sato, Y. Iso, T. Isobe, Fluorescence solvatochromism of carbon dot dispersions prepared from phenylenediamine and optimization of red emission, Langmuir 35 (2019) 15257-15266.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots-based dual-mode nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and fluoroquinolones

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kundu, B. Maity, S. Basu, Rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots-based dual-mode nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and fluoroquinolones, ACS Appl. Bio Mater. 8 (2022) 4764-4776.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Kim, Y. Park, S. Han, W. Park, M. Kim, K. Kim, et al., Radiative and non-radiative decay pathways in carbon nanodots toward bioimaging and photodynamic therapy, Nanomaterials 12 (2021) 70.
J.R. Lakowicz, Principles of fluorescence spectroscopy: Springer; 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comprehensive multispectroscopic analysis on the interaction and corona formation of human serum albumin with gold/silver alloy nanoparticles
